sections.heading: Follow-Up
sections.content: Continued long-term surveillance is essential because 13%–50% of patients will develop another SCC within 5 years, representing at least a 10-fold increase in risk compared to the general population. Patients with prior SCC are also at increased risk of developing cutaneous melanoma and BCC. Close follow-up during the first 2 years is critical due to the peak recurrence period. Most recurrences occur within 2 years of initial therapy. Local recurrence rates vary by treatment modality (e.g., 3.1% for primary CSCCs treated with Mohs). Recurrent tumors are at higher risk of metastasis. Risk factors for recurrence after treatment include larger subclinical extension, more Mohs stages, perineural invasion, and certain histologic features.

timeline.title: Low-risk patients
timeline.body: every 3–12 months for 2 years, then every 6–12 months for 3 years, then annually for life

timeline.title: High-risk patients
timeline.body: every 3–6 months for 2 years, then every 6–12 months for 3 years, then annually for life

timeline.title: Very-high-risk patients
timeline.body: every 3–6 months for 2 years, then every 6 months for 3 years, then every 6–12 months for life

late_effects.name: Secondary malignancies screening
late_effects.detail: Screening for secondary malignancies, especially in patients who received prior radiation therapy.

late_effects.name: Long-term radiation sequelae
late_effects.detail: Monitoring for long-term radiation sequelae: telangiectasia, fibrosis, non-healing ulcers, necrosis, decreased sensation, cataracts.

late_effects.name: Functional and cosmetic outcomes
late_effects.detail: Assessment of functional and cosmetic outcomes after surgery or radiation.

late_effects.name: Treatment-related toxicities
late_effects.detail: Surveillance for treatment-related toxicities from systemic therapies (e.g., endocrinopathies from immunotherapy).
